transcript: Cool. Well, thanks so much for coming. Appreciate you being here. I think there's some competition somewhere else for this talk, so thank you so much. So yeah, so the Solana vision, right, is basically to create these internet capital markets where you can have people from all over the world create applications, transact with each other, um, potentially accommodate all kinds of economic activity. But for that to happen, right, the network underneath also needs to perform at a certain level. So today I want to look at a bit where is Solana right now in terms of its performance and capacities and what are the main changes that are coming up that really will get this network to the place where it's sort of fit for the Solana vision. Um, so yeah, briefly about ourselves. So we've been deeply involved in Solana since the very beginning. Met Toly and Raj in like 2018, invested in Solana, we were there at the very first testnets, we were a Genesis Validator, helped also patch a bunch of vulnerabilities before mainnet, including one that allowed someone to mint lots of Solana. Um, we built a liquid... the first liquid staking solution on Solana that didn't end up surviving, but we did a bunch of the first MEV research. And today we, you know, we run a whole bunch of different validators and we do a lot of quantitative experiments as well where we measure performance and compare different configurations. And we'll talk a bit about that. So first of all, high level, where is Solana today? So we have seen Solana really improve tremendously in the last years. Even from the beginning, Solana had these ideas of 400 millisecond block times, but it wasn't the reality for years. It really was like 600 milliseconds, 500 milliseconds. And it took years to get us to the point where actually the network, if you look at this year, was really consistently below those 400 millisecond target. So tremendous improvement on that side. We've also had skip rate, right, in the past we had a lot of issues with stability, blocks being skipped, I mean downtime as well. So that has gotten way better and today, you know, skipping blocks has become a rare event. And of course, Solana has consistently high economic activity, a lot of transactions. So I think today it's around like, you know, 400 transactions from users, right, per block. And uh, definitely probably the most used of all of the blockchains in terms of economic activity happening. Um, but there's challenges as well. So at the moment, right, the economic activity on Solana is at a low point in the last two years. So MEV and priority fees are pretty low. Uh, you can see this chart here. And of course we had a crazy boom earlier this year, uh, sort of with the whole meme coins, Trump, there was an enormous amount, like the most ever, but now it's got... it's retrieved a lot. And that creates a lot of challenges, you know, including pressure on validator earnings and um, yeah. And one of the most interesting things happening right now is that we're seeing this explosion of custom schedulers. So maybe it's worth talking a little bit about what that is and why it matters. So if a validator is up there and producing a block, they have a certain amount of time and they receive lots of transactions and in that time they can process these transactions. And they can do this in different ways. And in the past nobody really did that, you know, there was just the default software and it processed things in one way, but today we are seeing a sort of divergence in different ways. So we see here like at the top one, that's the default client, you know, that just processes things at the beginning as quickly as possible and then it sort of tails off. But there's been other ones, for example, that um, will try to wait and then process all of them in the end, which can generate more revenues. Or there's at the bottom you can see uh, this is the Jito BAM one that tries to have a regular cadence and and basically process things at like small ticks throughout the block. So we see these different things, which is interesting because it gives a way to differentiate and create higher APY, um, but at the same time it creates unpredictability for application developers. But, you know, Solana's culture has always been one of radical innovation and just reinventing itself and pushing the limits. And you know, despite all of the success of Solana, the ultimate vision is still far away. And the network is also still far away from being able to accommodate that vision. And so if you look at actually a lot of the roadmap today of Solana, it's very ambitious and it's really completely changing how Solana works. And so I'm going to talk a bit about these changes and I think it's going to be tremendously interesting how that's going to play out. And it's going to have very big implications for validators, for application developers, for users, and hopefully for Solana holders, you know, if it makes the whole thing much more successful. Um, so a few of the main changes coming up. One is Alpenglow. So Alpenglow is a complete rewrite of the consensus algorithm. And, you know, it will reduce the time to finality by a lot. And Alpenglow also kind of facilitates then decreasing the slot time. And decreasing slot time has some benefits since that should reduce MEV a bit, especially as a percentage of the overall trading volume and activity. So that could be a positive change. And another one which, I don't know how many people are aware of that, but today validators, they have to send transactions to vote on the state of the chain and they have to pay fees for that. And uh, so if you're a small... and there's like a fixed cost of about one Solana a day. And if you're a big validator then you basically get fees from all the other validators voting on the chain. So it's a bit of a weird thing where the small ones are paying to the big ones. It really doesn't make any sense. So that is also something that's going away with Alpenglow where validators will no longer have to pay fees uh for their vote transactions. So that's one change. DoubleZero is another one. Um, DoubleZero is basically trying to create this high-performance fiber network uh to replace the internet uh routing. Um, we recently ran an experiment with this where we sort of tested how well it worked. Uh, it helped on the low uh far away peers to reduce latency. On the short ones not yet, although I think they they are working on changes there. But I think the the thing with DoubleZero really will be to give faster access to blockspace. Um, I think especially for RPCs, right, you could have better RPCs so that um, you know, you can you can read and write to the blockchain faster. Um, but let's now talk about probably the two most important changes that are coming and also the most complicated ones. So one is Proposer-Builder Separation. Or I would say there's a question whether that's coming and to what extent. But basically so far in Solana, right, we've had that the validators would get the transactions, create the blocks, and then they would also engage in consensus to basically create the valid chain. Um, but in Ethereum for example, it's been working differently for years where you have one party that gets the transactions, creates the blocks, and then the validators basically receive, you know, different block proposals from the builders and they choose whichever one pays the most. So you have this separation of the block builder and the validator. So, you know, that has pros and cons. Uh, but there are attempts to creating something like that on Solana. So first of all Jito uh has uh created BAM, and BAM basically has uh a sort of... it's also trying to address this earlier issue where you have different schedulers, right? So they have uh one scheduler that processes transaction in a specific way and that runs in a trusted execution environment so somebody can verify that it's really running like that. And then as a validator you would just receive um the blocks from these BAM nodes. Uh so, you know, there's pros and cons to this, right? First of all, it would standardize how the block... how these BAM blocks work. So that might be better for someone who wants to create a DEX or some financial applications on it. Um, at the same time it would, you know, will reduce competition if it really succeeds, reduce the role of the validators. Um, so, you know, it's a big a big open question. It also creates a different kind of centralization because now you have a few nodes that basically would produce all the blocks. And a big question here is what's the threshold at which BAM even starts working? Because, you know, if 10% of nodes use BAM it's probably pretty like doesn't really make a difference. So you might need to get to a very high percentage of the network adopting BAM for it to bring benefits. Uh, and then there's also uh Harmonic. Now not so much is known about Harmonic, but I think what Harmonic is trying to do is more like what is the case on Ethereum where you can have different builders, they compete with each other, uh, you know, they can have different methods, um, propose a block and then a validator can choose which blocks to take. So that might have the advantage that there is more innovation, uh, more diversity. Um, but it uh also means probably less predictability and probably the sort of profit maximizing schedulers uh would would win there. Um, which, you know, has its pros and cons. So that's Proposer-Builder Separation. But then I think the actually the really biggest change is going to be uh Multiple Concurrent Proposers. I think Max Resnick is doing a talk about this later, but that's really I think one of the most uh ambitious changes in like all of blockchain uh sort of architecture. And the idea here is that instead of having one leader that processes transaction, creates a block, you have many of them. How many TBD, maybe, you know, a few, 15, 20, 100 I've heard. Um, that do it all at the same time. And they basically create partial blocks at the same time. And that would have some really profound implication because all of these MEV games, this scheduling thing we saw earlier, it all depends on waiting and taking some time and then doing something. But if, you know, if 10 people all try to create a block at the same time, they get the same transaction, and then whoever includes it first sort of gets the rewards, it makes it very difficult to do that. So I think this will completely change the MEV landscape. Like even these previous things like BAM, Harmonic, they wouldn't work in the same way. Like it it will really totally uh change things. But I think in a good way. I think there will be a lot of upsides coming. But of course it's early and, you know, the second order effect of this is still uh unclear. Um, I think the latest I heard is maybe like early 2027 or something that this could come. Um, and and the other thing is also cool is like, okay, if you have lots of leaders across the world, then if you send a transaction there you get a quick confirmation, it could sort of reduce latency of the whole network. So those are I think the most important changes. So I think if of those things happen really we'll have a completely different network. It will look completely different. And it might actually be the kind of network that's now able to accommodate a massive amount of economic activity that's able to have also trading and exchanges work on Solana at a really high level of performance. And then it can live up to the internet capital markets vision. So, thanks so much.

enrichment.generated_query: Chorus One Solana Breakpoint 2025 announcement official blog OR technical docs

tags.impact_reason: The talk discusses Solana's network performance, validator earnings, and MEV, providing a solid update on the state of the infrastructure.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a 'Futuristic Data Loom' as the semantic anchor because the story is about 'rearchitecting consensus' and 'how blocks are built'. A loom is a perfect metaphor for weaving individual threads (transactions) into a coherent fabric (a block). The 'Multi-Proposer' concept is visualized as multiple robotic shuttles working in parallel, creating a more robust and orderly weave, which directly contrasts with the chaotic tangles of 'MEV games'. For the diegetic text, I chose '400ms' as it's a key performance metric highlighted in the transcript, representing the new, achieved speed and efficiency. It is integrated as a holographic readout on the machine itself, reinforcing the 'NASDAQ-scale' precision.
visual_idea.description: A monumental, futuristic data loom, constructed from polished obsidian and brushed steel, operates in a vast, dark server hall. Multiple robotic arms move with precision, weaving thousands of glowing fiber-optic strands (transactions) into a luminous, perfectly ordered digital tapestry. The lighting is low-key and cinematic, focusing on the intricate weaving process. Integrated into the machine's chassis, a subtle, sharp holographic projection displays the operational speed: '400ms'. The background is filled with the deep, out-of-focus racks of a NASDAQ-scale data center.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A monumental, futuristic data loom, towering vertically, constructed from seamless, highly reflective polished obsidian and finely textured brushed steel, dominates a vast, dark server hall. Multiple articulated robotic arms extend with surgical precision, meticulously weaving thousands of vibrant, glowing fiber-optic strands, representing digital transactions, into an intricate, perfectly ordered luminous digital tapestry. The intricate weaving process is the central focus. Integrated flawlessly into the machine's obsidian chassis, a sharp, subtle holographic projection, shimmering with a cool blue hue, clearly displays the operational speed: '400ms'. The low-key cinematic volumetric lighting casts dramatic shadows and highlights, with visible light rays cutting through the gloom, emphasizing the scale and complexity. The background consists of deep, out-of-focus racks of a NASDAQ-scale data center, fading into darkness, highlighting the foreground machine. Composed for a vertical 3:4 aspect ratio, emphasizing epic height and monumental scale, with substantial negative space above the loom to underscore its towering presence. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, cinematic volumetric lighting, dramatic contrast, unreal engine 5.

We see several glowing fiber-optic threads, each a different color, being perfectly woven together by the tip of a polished metallic shuttle. The focus is on the flawless precision of the weave, with the shallow depth of field blurring the surrounding machinery into abstract shapes and light.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A macro shot focusing on the intricate point of convergence on the data loom, perfectly composed for a square 1:1 aspect ratio. The scene is dominated by several vibrant, glowing fiber-optic threads, each a different color, being perfectly woven together with flawless precision by the sleek, polished metallic tip of a shuttle. The surrounding complex machinery of the data loom features textures reminiscent of polished obsidian, exhibiting deep, dark reflectivity that catches cool tech-blue reflections and sharp highlights. Cinematic volumetric lighting permeates the space, creating dramatic, low-key light and shadow effects, with soft, visible dust motes dancing in the air, emphasizing the intricate, hyper-detailed mechanical components. A shallow depth of field masterfully blurs the background machinery into abstract shapes and ethereal light, ensuring the central focus remains on the precise weave and the glowing threads, filling the entire frame with rich material detail and hyper-realistic textures. 16k, octane render, unreal engine 5, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, ultra HD, ultra quality, cinematic lighting, macro photography.

editorial.headline: Solana Rearchitects Consensus: Alpenglow and Multi-Proposers End MEV Games
editorial.standfirst: The network is undergoing its most radical infrastructure overhaul since Genesis, moving beyond simple speed to a complete rewrite of how blocks are built and propagated. For institutional investors, the shift to Multiple Concurrent Proposers represents the end of current MEV strategies and the beginning of true 'NASDAQ-scale' throughput.
key_takeaways: **Alpenglow Upgrade**: A complete consensus rewrite targeting <150ms finality and eliminating validator vote fees, drastically altering the economics for smaller node operators.

**Infrastructure Wars**: **DoubleZero** is deploying a dedicated fiber network to bypass public internet routing, while **Jito BAM** and **Harmonic** compete to monopolize block building via Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) and competitive markets.
**The 2027 Endgame**: **Multiple Concurrent Proposers (MCP)** will replace the single-leader model with 15-100 simultaneous leaders, effectively neutralizing latency-based MEV strategies.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
While Solana has successfully stabilized block times consistently below 400ms, a quiet crisis of economic activity is driving a radical re-engineering of the network's core. With user transactions stabilizing around 400 per block and priority fees at two-year lows, the validator economy is fracturing under the pressure of "custom schedulers." Validators are increasingly diverging from default clients, utilizing specialized software to manipulate transaction ordering for higher APY. This fragmentation creates unpredictability for developers but signals a maturing, cutthroat market for blockspace supremacy.

The immediate response to these scaling bottlenecks lies in two massive infrastructure upgrades: **Alpenglow** and **DoubleZero**. **Alpenglow** represents a total rewrite of the consensus algorithm, designed to slash time-to-finality and, crucially, eliminate the daily operational tax of vote transactions for validators. Simultaneously, the **DoubleZero** initiative is building a high-performance fiber network to replace standard internet routing for validators, offering a hardware-accelerated fast lane for block propagation. These upgrades are not merely optimizations; they are necessary steps to support the network's "Internet Capital Markets" thesis.

However, the true "alpha" lies in the impending war for block production. We are witnessing a bifurcation in Proposer-Builder Separation (PBS). **Jito BAM** is pushing for standardization via Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs)—effectively creating a trusted, centralized build pipeline—while **Harmonic** attempts to replicate Ethereum’s competitive builder market. Yet, both may be interim solutions. The endgame, championed by **Anza**'s **Max Resnick**, is **Multiple Concurrent Proposers (MCP)**. Slated for early 2027, MCP completely dismantles the current single-leader paradigm.

Under MCP, 15 to 100 leaders will produce partial blocks simultaneously. This shift is profound: it destroys the "waiting game" strategies currently used to extract MEV. If a validator waits to snipe a transaction, another proposer will simply include it first. In this new regime, speed is the only metric that matters. For institutional capital, this transition marks the moment Solana moves from a high-speed blockchain to a genuine global exchange layer, capable of accommodating the frequency and volume of traditional financial markets without the latency drag of sequential processing.
